2005-04-01.title: News Analysis: Schiavo's legacy is a national debate
Iraqi rebels press attacks on Shiites
Son Assumes Duties of Monaco's Ailing Leader
Schröder Asserts His Authority On Ending China Arms Embargo
2005-04-01.abstract: Chancellor Gerhard Schroder of Germany, faced with surge of opposition in Parliament to his proposal to lift arms embargo on China, claims he has sole authority to speak for Germany on issue, whatever Parliament might say; Schroder and Pres Jacques Chirac of France are leading advocates for lifting arms embargo, which would have to be approved by European Union as a whole; idea is opposed by United States and several European countries; unlike Schroder, Chirac's position has occasioned no particular protest in France (M)

2005-04-01.title: A Final Verdict on Prewar Intelligence Is Still Elusive
2005-04-01.abstract: Analysis: presidential commission on intelligence leaves little doubt that Pres Bush and top aides got what they wanted, not what they needed, when they were told that Saddam Hussein had threatening arsenal of illicit weapons; report states that intelligence analysts worked in environment that did not encourage skepticism about the conventional wisdom; latest and presumably last official review of one of worst intelligence failures of modern times leaves unresoveld what may be biggest question of all: who is accountable, and will they ever be hold to account for letting mere assumptions 'harden into presumptions,' according to Judge Laurence H Silberman, who chaired panel; photo of Silberman and co-chair Charles Robb (M)

2005-04-02.title: Tomfoolery in News Articles Is a Proud April 1 Tradition Among the Media in Britain
2005-04-02.abstract: Fake April 1 article is British newspaper tradition, befitting country where news media revel in not taking themselves too seriously; examples of several April 1 articles and BBC hoax report cited; photo (M)

2005-04-02.title: Ex-C.I.A. Chief Denies Knowing of Doubt About Defector's Word
2005-04-02.abstract: Former CIA director George Tenet says he was never told of foreign intelligence service's grave doubts about reliability of Curveball, code name of Iraqi defector who was important source of information on Iraq's purported biological weapons program; mounts vigorous defense of his role in episode at core of presidential commission's report of prewar CIA reporting on weapons; Curveball provided information that Saddam Hussein was building network of mobile biological weapons laboratories; story, used by former secretary of state Colin Powell to bolster case for war at UN turned out to be untrue (M)

2005-04-02.title: Hard to believe the news? It must be April 1

2005-04-03.title: Help Wanted: China Finds Itself With a Labor Shortage
2005-04-03.abstract: China is facing shortage of about two million workers in Guangdong and Fujian provinces, at heart of export-driven economy; young migrant workers coveted by factories are gaining bargaining power; current shortfalls may reflect long-term trend that is already bringing wage pressures and could erode China's position as world's dominant low-cost producer; two decades of family planning have made China one of most rapidly aging countries in world; other regions of China offer growing competition for Guangdong, and families do not want their only child far from home; photo, map (M)

2005-04-03.title: Krakow and Beyond: Prayers, Tributes and Awe: Toronto; A Leader Lost
2005-04-03.abstract: In a small neighborhood of Toronto known as Little Poland, people gathered to pray at St. Casimir's Catholic Church, where in the basement children attend the Pope John Paul II Judo School. The Rev. Janusz Blazejak said the pope was an important figure in the tightly knit Polish community, and represented for many a tie to their home country.     ''What the pope means to our community is what the pope means to Poland, maybe a little more,'' he said, sitting in his rectory office. ''I can't imagine the world without the pope. It's a great loss and a sadness. We shouldn't be ashamed of the tears. God gave us a wonderful gift. Polish people will not forget him.''

2005-04-03.title: Abbas Moves to Control West Bank Security Forces
2005-04-03.abstract: Palestinian Pres Mahmud Abbas, having fired police commander in Ramallah, accepts resignation of security chief in West Bank following attack on his headquarters and rampage through Ramallah by Al Aksa Martyrs Brigades in protest against demand that they either join regular security forces or disarm; Abbas must quickly assert his authority and restore public order or he and his Fatah faction risk losing ground to radical Hamas group in July parliamentary elections; photo (M)

2005-04-03.title: Krakow and Beyond: Prayers, Tributes and Awe: Mexico; Celebrating Restoration
2005-04-03.abstract: Mexico was the first stop of Karol Wojtyla's world odyssey as pope. It was 1979 and he said he had come to rescue a ''persecuted church.''      Today, the church and its clergy are openly embraced in Mexico. Beneath a statue of John Paul at the Basilica of Guadalupe, Catholics like 65-year-old Anselma Torres expressed hope that the pontiff's death would mark a renewal.

2005-04-03.title: Quebec Is Shedding Image as Hotbed of Political Rest
2005-04-03.abstract: Politics in predominantly French-speaking Quebec are more lively than at any time since last separatist referendum in 1995; college students have been on strike for last month, demanding government reverse plan to transform $80 million of student grants into loans; college teachers are pressing contract demands, elementary teachers and government workers are threatening action, and unions are organizing workers; disconnected developments and Liberal Party scandal benefit separatist parties; photos (M)

2005-04-04.title: U.S. rejects visa application by Margaret Thatcher's son
Militants Threaten Kashmir's New Bus Service
2005-04-04.abstract: Threats from militant groups operating on Indian side of Kashmir sow fear among would-be passengers just days before planned resumption of bus service across divided Kashmir; Indian security forces are promising beefed-up protection; four obscure self-described insurgency groups responsible for 15-year fight against Indian rule in Kashmir have threatened to turn buses into coffins, leaving long-hopeful travelers confused and anxious; bus route goes from Srinagar on Indian side to Muzaffarbad on Pakistani side; Indian Prime Min Manmohan Singh will see off first bus in Srinagar; map (M)

2005-04-04.title: Pope's Visitors Saw Serenity in Final Hours
2005-04-04.abstract: Pope Pope John Paul II lay in wide, white-blanketed bed in sun-filled bedroom in final hours of his life; visitors to his bedside and official Vatican reports portray him as tranquil and comfortable in face of death; photo (M)

2005-04-04.title: Reflections of Those, and on Those, Who Met Him
2005-04-04.abstract: Pope John Paul II's humility is mentioned over and over by friends and acquaintances in loving television tributes to him; but his death has not always brought out humility in others; reporters, commentators and clergymen alike can be heard telling personal anecdotes about pope that, mostly, reflect favorably on themselves (TV Watch column) (M)

2005-04-04.title: Syrians Promise to Quit Lebanon by Month's End
2005-04-04.abstract: United Nations envoy to Lebanon Terje Roed-Larsen says Syria's leaders promise to pull out all military and intelligence forces from Lebanon by end of April, one month before scheduled nation-wide elections there; makes announcement after meeting with Syrian Pres Bashar al-Assad and Foreign Min Farouk al-Sharaa; Syrian forces have occupied parts of Lebanon since 1976; their withdrawal is one of central demands of Lebanese opposition, whose supporters have staged huge rallies over past six weeks; assassination of former Prime Min Rafik Hariri on Feb 14 and protests that followed set in motion series of events that have been transforming Lebanese politics; many Lebanese blame Syrian government, and its clients in Lebanese government, for Hariri's killing; Lebanese opposition leaders say continuing political impasse may result in delay in nationwide elections; accuse pro-Syrian Prime Min Omar Karami and his Syrian backers of trying to put off elections for as long as possible to extend life of current pro-Syrian Parliament; photo (M)

2005-04-05.title: A Brooklyn Retiree's Last Trip to See a Special Friend in Rome
2005-04-05.abstract: Stella Gryziec, Polish-American, recalls day when Cardinal Karol Wojtyla, who became Pope John Paul II, visited her church in Greenpoint, Brooklyn, in 1969 (M)

2005-04-05.title: Pope John Paul Appraised as Pope, Not Rock Star
2005-04-05.abstract: Critic's Notebook: panoramic television coverage of death of Pope John Paul II has described him, almost compulsively, as a rock star; what is harder to divine in television images is his identity as Roman Catholic (M)

2005-04-05.title: Accommodations Improve for Arriving Cardinals
2005-04-05.abstract: Behind Vatican's guarded gates, 117 cardinals arriving from all around world to elect pope's successor will stay at Santa Marta Residence, $20 million hotel built by pope for that purpose; map (M)

2005-04-05.title: A Swelling Throng Waits Patiently for a Last Glimpse
2005-04-05.abstract: Public viewing of Pope John Paul II will be enormous operation fraught with logistical troubles; about two million people are expected to churn through line by time he is buried on Apr 8; photos (M)

2005-04-06.title: Vatican to end public viewings of Pope's body
Iraqi parties break a deadlock on candidates
Mourning for pope swells into pilgrimage
New rules set on borders with Mexicoand Canada
Sharon meets settlers to press Gaza pullout

2005-04-07.title: At Least 16 People Killed When U.S. Military Helicopter Crashes in Afghanistan
2005-04-07.abstract: American military helicopter crashes in bad weather near Ghazni, Afghanistan, killing at least 16 people, most of them presumed to be military personnel; United States military spokeswoman says some victims may be non-Americans; recovery operation is under way (M)

2005-04-07.title: Dutch citizen acquitted of plotting terror attacks
Names of the Dead
2005-04-07.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of four service members in Iraq (S)

2005-04-07.title: One Million Wait to View the Pope as Line Is Cut Off
2005-04-07.abstract: Estimated one million pilgrams stand in streets around St Peter's Basilica, waiting to view body of Pope John Paul II; surge is so great that Italian safety officials close down line; foreign dignitaries begin arriving to pay their respects and attend funeral on Apr 8; Pres Bush and wife Laura visit St Peter's Basilica with former presidents Bush and Clinton, Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and Bush's chief of staff Andrew H Card Jr; despite its size, crowd filling streets near Vatican is calm; weeklong celebration of life of John Paul II is clearly pleasing to church officials--even if it raises questions about whether church can sustain such fervent enthusiasm under next pope; photos (L)

2005-04-07.title: Bush Nominee for U.N. Post Faces Hurdles
2005-04-07.abstract: Senate Foreign Relations Committee may not approve John R Bolton's nomination as ambassador to United Nations; committee will hear testimony from Carl W Ford Jr, former chief of State Dept's Bureau of Intelligence and Research, who will describe his clashes with Bolton over what he regarded as Bolton's intimidation of intelligence officials; committee is also seeking testimony from two intelligence officials, one a top CIA analyst, about Bolton's alleged efforts to have them replaced for disagreeing with him over weapons programs in several countries; panel is also reviewing previous allegations that Bolton tried to suppress information undercutting Bush administration's contentions about unconventional weapons; Sen Lincoln Chafee, Republican member of panel, has reserved final judgment on Bolton's nomination (M)

2005-04-08.title: Anglicans Ponder Passing of Their Identity and Power
2005-04-08.abstract: Some historians and columnists in Britain claim that English response to death of Pope John Paul II shows that Protestant England is dead; this impression is reinforced by Prince Charles' decision to postpone his wedding to Camilla Parker Bowles to permit Prime Min Tony Blair and Most Rev Rowan Williams, head of Church of England, to attend papal burial rather than royal wedding; Church of England--part of Anglican Communion--has long been in decline; number of Anglican clegty members has fallen from 18,000 in laet 1960's to about 9,500 (M)

2005-04-08.title: China Says Jailed Researcher Now Faces New Inquiry
Hong Kong chief defends Beijing appeal
Indonesia extends handto its strange bedfellow

2005-04-09.title: Early Morning Gatherings Offer Communal Farewells to Pope
2005-04-09.abstract: Over 1,000 people watch Polish-language simulcast of Pope John Paul's funeral on giant screen in Five Holy Martyrs Catholic Church in Chicago; John Paul visited the church on Oct 5, 1979 and in 1976 and 1969 before he became pope (M)

2005-04-09.title: Gunmenopen fire on backers of Iraqi cleric
Fear and Violence Accompany a Deadly Virus Across Angola
2005-04-09.abstract: Death toll in Angola from epidemic caused by Marburg virus rises to 174 as aid workers in Uige report that terrified people had attacked them and number of health workers had fled out of fear of catching the disease; international health officials say epidemic, already largest outbreak of Marburg virus, shows no signs of abating; World Health Organization dispatches surveillance teams to country's northern provinces; there is no cure or vaccine for highly contagious Ebola-like virus; photo (M)

2005-04-09.title: U.S. to expand meaning of enemy, group says
A Time for Mourning, but Also for Study and Very Quiet Politics
2005-04-09.abstract: Pope John Paul II's funeral Mass on Friday began nine days of spiritual mourning, but it also opened a political chapter -- the subtle campaigning before the gathering of cardinals in a conclave starting April 18 to elect a successor.    Each day of mourning is marked by a Mass, several of which will be celebrated by cardinals -- whose voices will be listened to closely for any hints about what direction they are moving in. The first is on Saturday afternoon, to be celebrated by Cardinal Francesco Marchisano, a career Vatican official who is the archpriest of St. Peter's Basilica.

2005-04-10.title: A Disease Devastating to Sheep Threatens Spanish Bullfighting
2005-04-10.abstract: Many of Spain's bulls are confined to ranches under quarantine aimed at halting spread of blue-tongue disease, which rarely harms cattle but can be devastating to sheep, which are major part of Spain's $9 billion livestock industry; National Assn of Organizers of Bullfighting Spectacles calls current measures grave crisis; government says quarantine may be broken so that bulls can travel to fights, but only under conditions industry calls prohibitively costly; Spaniards spend more than $1 billion per year to watch estimated 17,000 bullfights (M)

2005-04-10.title: Rally by Israeli Rightists Is Foiled
Mexican Aide Is Freed
2005-04-10.abstract: Nahum Acosta Lugo, former aide to Mexican Pres Vicente Fox, is released from prison after judge rules there is not enough evidence to hold him on charges of leaking information to lieutenant of notorious drug dealer Joaquin Guzman about president's trips (S)a

2005-04-10.title: India and China Are Poised to Share Defining Moment
2005-04-10.abstract: Chinese Prime Min Wen Jiabao begins four-day visit to India; China is India's second largest trade partner, and trade is growing by 30 percent each year to estimated $14 billion this year; there is talk of free trade agreement and joint oil exploration, but competition is as much byword as cooperation; World Bank credits their combined growth with cutting share of world's population living in extreme poverty to 20 percent in 2001 from 40 percent two decades earlier, but China enjoys good 15-year head start in economic reform over India; political delays slow development in India while China's future success may depend on democratic reforms; photo (M)

2005-04-10.title: Riot Police Called In to Calm Anti-Japanese Protests in China
2005-04-10.abstract: Mass demonstrations in Beijing against Japan turn unruly and protesters try to storm Japanese ambassador's residence before riot police break up confrontation; estimated 10,000 demonstrators join march calling for boycott of Japanese goods, making it one of largest protest events authorized by government in years, but gatherings at embassy and ambassador's residence seem to be unauthorized; Japan's vice foreign minister makes official protest against violence (M)

2005-04-11.title: A week of dubious witnesses in Jackson's trial
Bolton pledges to work with others to improve U.N.
Four killed, hundreds trapped in factory collapse in Bangladesh
Senators to gauge spy chief's intentions
Bologna Journal; Some Lebanese Villagers Won't Cheer as Syrians Leave
2005-04-11.abstract: Many Lebanese villagers are more ambivalent about withdrawal of Syrian troops than size of anti-Syrian demonstrations in Beirut would suggest; say Syrian troops brought peace and stability when they entered Lebanon in 1976, mainly by keeping warring Lebanese factions from killing one another; say Syrian soldiers treated them respectfully and kept mostly to themselves; photo; map (M)

2005-04-12.title: Bush Supports Plan by Sharon for a Withdrawal From Gaza
2005-04-12.abstract: Pres Bush and Prime Min Ariel Sharon of Israel differ openly over Israel's intention to expand settlement in West Bank, but Bush gives Sharon robust backing for his plan to withdraw from Gaza this summer; two meet at Bush's ranch in Crawford, Tex; Bush hails Sharon for pursuing 'courageous initiative' to pull Israeli settlers and security forces out of Gaza Strip and small portions of West Bank; his words of support come as Sharon heads toward wrenching confrontation with some Jewish settlers and their allies as he moves them, perhaps forcibly, out of Gaza; he has described political atmosphere in Israel as akin to civil war because of deep disagreements over withdrawal plan; but Sharon is adamant about plans to build new housing to establish unbroken presence from Jerusalem to West Bank settlement of Maale Adumin, community of 30,000 people; planned building could cut most north-south links between Palestinians in West Bank and make it extremely difficult for them to gain access to East Jerusalem, which they hope to make capital of their eventual state; White House says framework for peace talks, so-called road map, bars such construction; photo (M)

2005-04-12.title: Nominee to U.N. Defends Record at Senate Panel
2005-04-12.abstract: John R Bolton, at his Senate confirmation hearing, rebuts attacks from Democrats on his fitness to serve as ambassador to United Nations; pledges to bring about 'close partnership' with organization and denies ever trying to get anyone dismissed for disagreeing with him on intelligence matters; Sen Lincoln Chafee, lone doubting Republican on Senate Foreign Relations Committee, says he is generally satisfied with Bolton's answers and is 'inclined' to vote to approve him; Bolton seeks to turn his criticism of UN to his advantage, saying his views make him right person to help restore credibility to organization and make it more effective; next person scheduled to testify about Bolton will be Democrats' star witness Carl W Ford Jr, former State Dept official who clashed with Bolton in 2002; photos (M)

2005-04-12.title: In Africa, church carves its own distinctive path
Life on hold for Sri Lankan tsunami victims

2005-04-13.title: Sharon Asks U.S. to Pressure Iran to Give Up Its Nuclear Program
2005-04-13.abstract: Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon, visiting Pres Bush's Texas ranch, reportedly spreads out photos of Iranian nuclear sites as he urges US to step up pressure on Iran to give up all elements of its nuclear program; reports Israeli intelligence shows Iran near 'point of no return' on developing weapon; he gives no indication that Israel is preparing to act alone, prospect raised by Vice Pres Cheney; Sharon warns European negotiators are softening position and might allow Iran to hold onto uranium-enrichment technology; information is not new but Sharon reportedly pressures Bush not to let talks drag on and says he has little confidence in outcome; photo of two (M)

2005-04-13.title: Thousands of Chinese Villagers Protest Factory Pollution
Serbia Moves a Step Closer to the European Union
2005-04-13.abstract: European Commission report says Serbia has improved its cooperation with war crimes tribunal in The Hague and recommends European Union nations start talks with federation of Serbia and Montenegro in first step toward EU membership (M)

2005-04-13.title: Rumsfeld Laces Talks to Iraqis With Optimism and Warnings
2005-04-13.abstract: Defense Secretary Donald H. Rumsfeld spent Tuesday in a whirlwind trip around Iraq that included ''town hall'' meetings with American troops outside the capital, talks with government officials in Baghdad, and a final stop here, at a Kurdish stronghold beneath snow-capped mountains where anti-Saddam Hussein forces plotted for years against the Iraqi dictator -- and against other Kurds.    Mr. Rumsfeld laced his message of American steadfastness with warnings to Iraqi leaders about the dangers that political cronyism and partisan purges posed for the effort to organize, equip and train a security force.

2005-04-13.title: Wen ends his visit with giants vowing to be cooperative

2005-04-14.title: A 3rd term would be his last, Blair says
Negroponte's cold war role is disclosed
Afghan Leader to Propose Strategic Ties With the U.S.
2005-04-14.abstract: Pres Hamid Karzai of Afghanistan says he will ask Pres Bush for long-term strategic relationship with United States that would include economic assistance as well as security guarantees and military cooperation; Karzai opens door to possibly allowing rights to Afghan airspace and moving troops through country; talks could even lead to undetermined number of American soldiers being based there (M)

2005-04-14.title: France Lets Terminally Ill Refuse Care, but Still Bans Euthanasia
2005-04-14.abstract: French legislators approve law that would permit terminally ill patients to refuse treatment in favor of death, but stops short of legalizing euthanasia; law is quiet coda to dramatic mercy killing of severely disabled man in 2003; it allows families to end life support for patients in coma, as in Terri Schiavo case, and permits doctors to prescribe pain medication for terminally ill patients even if those drugs may hasten death; it does not allow doctors to act to end patient's life, even at patient's request; law formalizes what has been practice in French hospitals but has rarely risen to level of public debate that such cases have caused in US (M)

2005-04-14.title: Elections hit snag as prime minister quits in Lebanon

2005-04-15.title: World Briefing | Europe: Scotland: First Royal Duty
2005-04-15.abstract: Great Britain's Prince Charles and his new bride Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall, interrupt their honeymoon at Scotland's Balmoral estate to attend opening of school playground at Ballater, Scotland; photo (S)

2005-04-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Colombia: Rebels Attack 5 Towns
2005-04-15.abstract: Colombian Revolutionary Armed Forces rebels attack five southern province towns and kill two police officers; rebels have increased intensity and frequency of attacks this year (S)

2005-04-15.title: Rebels asked for deal on tsunami aid
World Briefing | Europe: France: Champion (And Castrated) Horse Is Cloned
2005-04-15.abstract: Cryozootech, French biotechnical company, announces that world endurance champion horse Pieraz has been cloned; foal is 50 days old; photo (S)

2005-04-15.title: Chirac Goes on TV to Promote Europe's Constitution
2005-04-15.abstract: Pres Jacques Chirac appears on French television to urge voters to approve draft constitution for European Union; photo; struggles to explain why document of more than 850 pages would be relevant to daily lives in France, amid mounting evidence that it may not pass referendum; many in France fear more powerful union will threaten French social benefits and cause even higher unemployment; others are wary of expanding union that may include Muslim Turkey (M)

2005-04-16.title: Bush orders review of passport rule
Chirac's EU push sways few voters
A Sudanese City of Refugees With No Plans to Go Home
2005-04-16.abstract: Population of a refugee camp in Abushouk, Sudan, has grown by almost 50 percent since Dec 2004 to about 100,000 people; they are living solely on international aid; some aid workers are concerned that none of two million refugees in Darfur camps have any intention of leaving; map (M)

2005-04-16.title: Afghan Trips Over Rules He Upheld to U.S. Senate
2005-04-16.abstract: Rahim Bariek, Virginia financier from Afghanistan, is charged with operating illegal money-transmittal business; federal prosecutors say he used his business to send nearly $5 million to Iran, Pakistan and parts of Afghanistan that were under control of Taliban in 2001 and 2002; Bariek appeared as witness before Senate panel after Sept attacks and spoke of how important it was for business people like himself, who operate an informal money-exchange system known as hawala, to follow the law (M)

2005-04-17.title: Pakistani Politician Taken Into Custody
2005-04-17.abstract: Asif Ali Zardari, the husband of Benazir Bhutto, a former Pakistani prime minister, returned to the eastern Pakistan city of Lahore on Saturday and was immediately taken into custody by state security forces.    Mr. Zardari, 50, an opposition politician, had moved to Dubai to meet his wife and three children after being released on bail last November. Before that, he had spent eight years in a Pakistani prison awaiting trial on corruption and murder charges.

2005-04-17.title: Cardinals Align as Time Nears to Select Pope
2005-04-17.abstract: There was never doubt that Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger, the Vatican's hard-line defender of the faith, would have a strong hand in selecting the next pope. But in the days of prayer and politics before the conclave, which begins on Monday, he has emerged as perhaps the surprise central figure: the man who could become the 265th pope, choose him or be the one other cardinals knock from the running.     Any talk of who will become the next pope is guesswork, echoes from cardinals and their staffs sworn to silence about one of the world's most elite and secretive gatherings.

2005-04-17.title: Cardinals Finish Preparations for Conclave
2005-04-17.abstract: The cooks and elevator operators have been sworn to secrecy. A smokestack has been placed on the Sistine Chapel's roof. And on Saturday, the cardinals who will elect Pope John Paul II's successor held their last formal meeting.    Most details have been tied up ahead of Monday's conclave, the secret gathering of the princes of the church that will end when white smoke emerges to signal that they have chosen a new pope.

2005-04-17.title: China Allows More Protests in Shanghai Against Japan
2005-04-17.abstract: One day before a high-level Japanese visit aimed at defusing rising tensions between the countries, thousands of Chinese surrounded the Japanese Consulate here in an angry daylong demonstration.     After a three-hour march that began on the Shanghai riverfront, steadily gaining strength along a 10-mile route, well over 10,000 Chinese gathered at the consulate chanting anti-Japanese slogans and throwing a variety of missiles -- from heavy chunks of pavement to paint balloons and bottles -- at the consulate while several thousand law enforcement agents looked on passively.

2005-04-18.title: Cardinals assembled to elect a pope
No. 1 Tory campaigns with a lot of baggage
Discerning a New Course for World's Donor Nations
2005-04-18.abstract: World Bank, International Monetary Fund and British Prime Min Tony Blair have called for increased financial support to developng nations, particularly those in Africa, but impact is uncertain because United States has not joined this chorus; American aid to Africa has almost tripled in Bush years, but last year US gave almost as much development aid to Iraq, population 25 million, as it did to all of Africa, population 700 million; graph (M)

2005-04-18.title: Armenian-Turkish Dispute
2005-04-18.abstract: Turkish State Archive issues list of more than 523,000 Turks it says were killed by Armenians in Turkey from 1910 to 1922; move seems intended to counter historical record of genocide committed by Ottoman Turks against Armenians in and around 1915; Turkey is concerned that issue could hurt its chances to join European Union (S)

2005-04-19.title: Ministersin France clash over EU charter
200 held in ethnic unrest, Iran discloses
Tokyo to beef up security around China-related sites in Japan
Politicus: Howard's star falling as Blair's again climbs
Woman in custody after Paris hotel blaze

2005-04-20.title: From Roots in Wartime Germany to the Papacy
2005-04-20.abstract: Along with his subtle and powerful intellect lies a spiritual, almost mystical side rooted in Bavaria.

2005-04-20.title: Ecuador President Is Ousted After Protests
For America's Divided Roman Catholics, a New Disagreement
2005-04-20.abstract: Roman Catholics pour into cathedrals and parish churches across US to celebrate Masses of Thanksgiving for new pope, Benedict XVI, but choice of Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger as pope prompts strong disagreement over what he will mean for American church; photos (M)

2005-04-20.title: White or Black? Maybe Beige? As Smoke Detectors, the Anchors Were All Too Fallible
2005-04-20.abstract: Alessandra Stanley column assesses television coverage of election of new pope (M)

2005-04-21.title: Rice Tells Putin U.S. Is No Threat in Region
2005-04-21.abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice offers reassurances to Pres Vladimir V Putin of Russia and to listeners of popular Moscow radio station that United States would not supplant Russia's influence in its neighborhood despite growing American friendship with other countries in region; Rice continues to suggest that Russia do more to foster economic and political freedoms; says she told Putin that seizure of Yukos oil conglomerate and prosecution of its executives 'shook people's confidence' in Russia's transition to democracy and free economy; photo (M)

2005-04-21.title: Ecuador's Leader Flees and Vice President Replaces Him
2005-04-21.abstract: Pres Lucio Gutierrez of Ecuador flees presidential palace after Congress, meeting in special session, votes to remove him; Congress then swears in Vice Pres Alfredo Palacio to replace Gutierrez, who faced mounting street protests against what critics called illegal overhaul of supreme Court; Gutierrez becomes third president since 1997 to be ousted from power in Ecuador; Palacio does not say if he would call new elections; Brazil issues statement saying Gutierrez is in country's embassy in Quito and that he will be granted asylum in Brazil; photo (M)

2005-04-21.title: New Papacy Stirs Some Concern in the Arab Middle East
2005-04-21.abstract: Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger's elevation to papacy stirs some uneasiness in Arab Middle East, where many remember Pope John Paul II as champion of their cause who expressed sympathy for Palestinians, spoke against West's materialism and sought interfaith dialogue; some Arab analysts see return to more traditional relationship, citing Ratziner's remarks about Christian-Muslim competition for new adherents (M) traditional relationship (M)

2005-04-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: More Arrests In Attack On Energy Chief
2005-04-21.abstract: Two more men were detained on suspicion of involvement in the attempted murder of Anatoly B. Chubais, chief of Russia's electricity monopoly and the architect of Russia's privatization in the 1990's. Mr. Chubais, one of Russia's most controversial men, was ambushed in his armored BMW outside Moscow last month and survived both a roadside bomb and gunfire. A former military intelligence colonel was already under arrest in the case, and the Interfax news agency reported that the new suspects had served with him. The authorities released few other details.   C.J. Chivers (NYT)

2005-04-21.title: EU official demands appearance by Barroso

2005-04-22.title: Israel Takes a Step Closer to a 3-Week Delay in Leaving Gaza
2005-04-22.abstract: Israeli Defense Min Shaul Mofaz formally calls for three-week delay, until Aug 15, in government's plans to remove Jewish settlers from Gaza Strip and parts of West Bank; Prime Min Ariel Sharon is expected to agree; postponement was suggested by government official supervising withdrawal, so evacuation would not overlap with annual three-week Jewish mourning period; delay will also give government and security forces more time to work out unresolved issues; vast majority of 9,000 Gaza settlers oppose evacuation and have not cooperated with government efforts to relocate them; it is not clear where they will live, and government is scrambling to find housing (M)

2005-04-22.title: A cardinal's visit put boy on path to the Vatican
World Briefing | Middle East: Saudi Arabia: Last Round Of Voting
2005-04-22.abstract: The third and final round of voting in Saudi Arabia's landmark, if partial, municipal elections unrolled in the western regions of the country, including the holy cities of Mecca and Medina. As in the previous rounds on Feb. 10 and March 3, only men over 21 were voting for a total 244 council seats in contention. Nationwide, the decision to elect nearly 600 members of the 178 councils, with the other half appointed, was seen as a first, limited step toward meeting demands for greater public participation in decision-making in the absolute monarchy. Candidates backed by prominent Muslim clergymen dominated the elections in major cities in the previous rounds.   Neil MacFarquhar (NYT)

2005-04-22.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Still Trapped Outside Prison
2005-04-22.abstract: Andrés Manuel López Obrador, above at center, the popular left-wing mayor of Mexico City and front-runner for president, may be the only politician in the world trying to have himself put in jail. Yesterday he went with supporters -- and a throng of reporters -- to the courthouse where the federal case against him on contempt-of-court charges is being heard and demanded that his bail be revoked so he could be imprisoned. A judge refused. On Wednesday, federal prosecutors presented the charges -- that he ignored a court order in 2001 -- but robbed him of the chance to look like a martyr for democracy by having two allies of President Vicente Fox pay his bail. Mr. López Obrador says the charges are trumped up and intended to knock him off the presidential ballot next year. He has vowed to run from jail if necessary. Meanwhile, he seems to be holding his own in polls. A poll by the newspaper El Universal indicated that his lead over candidates from the other two major parties had grown by three percentage points, to 38 percent over all, from 35 percent in February, which was before the government announced it would prosecute him and possibly push him out of the presidential race next year.   James C. McKinley Jr. (NYT)

2005-04-23.title: Pope-to-Be Reopened Mexican Sex Abuse Inquiry
2005-04-23.abstract: While still a cardinal, the new pope reopened an investigation into allegations that the Mexican founder of an influential Catholic order had molested teenage students.

2005-04-23.title: Pope Has Gained the Insight to Address Abuse, Aides Say
2005-04-23.abstract: The man who is now pope was greatly responsible for deciding whether and how to discipline priests accused of sexual abuse.

2005-04-23.title: Marine to head Joint Chiefs of Staff
Briefly: 10 die in clashes in Jammu-Kashmir
Gold lures Mongolians from the land

2005-04-24.title: Promises ($60 for Instance) Fail to Rouse Iran's Voters
2005-04-24.abstract: With elections less than two months away, Iran's presidential hopefuls are scrambling to lure voters, promising everything from lowering the soaring prices of fruit and vegetables to normalizing relations with the country's longtime foe, the United States.    But much of the public is deeply apathetic. There is no real chance for a candidate who can spearhead the democratic changes once promised by the departing president, Mohammad Khatami, who proved unable to ward off the encroachment of the Guardian Council and its repressive agenda. Over the last eight years, the opposition has been marginalized as the judiciary has shut down more than 100 pro-change newspapers and journals and jailed dozens of advocates and intellectuals.

2005-04-24.title: Annan Remark on Oil Sales Draws Nods of Agreement
2005-04-24.abstract: When Secretary General Kofi Annan declared in an unguarded moment last week that Saddam Hussein made most of his money ''on the American and British watch'' rather than from the United Nations oil-for-food program, he was expressing a view that is widely held but seldom uttered at the organization as it tries to avoid clashing with Washington.    Speaking to former United Nations communications officials and thinking his comments were confidential, Mr. Annan said Mr. Hussein pocketed far more from sanctions-skirting oil sales to Jordan and Turkey, which were being monitored by the United States and Britain, than from the scandal-tainted relief program run by the Security Council. ''They were the ones who had interdiction,'' he said. ''Possibly they were also the ones who knew exactly what was going on, and the countries themselves decided to close their eyes to smuggling to Turkey and Jordan because they were allies.''

2005-04-24.title: Big Dig Called Safe for Cars, but Not So for Pocketbooks
2005-04-24.abstract: While officials say that the leak-prone Big Dig tunnel project is safe for motorists, they can give no assurance that taxpayers will not be soaked more to pay for repairs.    Kenneth Mead, the inspector general for the United States Department of Transportation, told a Congressional panel on Friday that years of scant independent financial oversight and the partnership forged by the Big Dig project manager and the state agency overseeing the plan could make assigning blame for problems with the tunnel difficult.

2005-04-24.title: China and Japan Leaders Pledge to Improve Relations
2005-04-24.abstract: The leaders of Japan and China pledged to improve ties Saturday after weeks of escalating disputes, easing tension but not resolving some critical problems besieging relations between East Asia's big powers.    After a 55-minute meeting on the sidelines of an Asia-Africa summit conference here, Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi of Japan said both sides had agreed to look beyond disagreements and focus on the future.

2005-04-24.title: British Election Debates Spotlight Immigration Fears
2005-04-24.abstract: The issue of immigration moved to the center of Britain's election debate on Friday but here, among the grocers of Surrey Street market and the downtown shopping malls, many people seemed to have already drawn their conclusions.    This town on London's southern fringe is widely known as the headquarters of the British Home Office immigration authority, located in a bleak high-rise called Lunar House.

2005-04-25.title: Rice and Cheney Are Said to Push Iraqi Politicians on Stalemate
2005-04-25.abstract: Bush administration abandons its hands-off approach to Iraqi politics; presses top Kurdish and Shiite politicians to end their stalemate over forming new government with personal appeals from Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and Vice Pres Dick Cheney; there is concern about spike in mayhem from emboldened insurgency as insurgents unleash devastating technique: twin double bombings at police academy in Tikrit and ice cream parlor in Shiite neighborhood of Baghdad that kills 21 and wounds scores more; in both attacks, second bomb detonates within minutes after first, killing and wounding policemen and bystanders rushing to care for victims of initial blasts; American and Iraqi officials are blaming delay in forming new government for rise in violence in recent weeks; impact of White House pressure on top Iraqi leaders is unclear; photo (M)

2005-04-25.title: Reminders of John Paul Are Everywhere in the Festivities
2005-04-25.abstract: Memory of John Paul II is evident in installation of his successor Benedict XVI, in ways profound and personal (M)

2005-04-25.title: Benedict XVI Is Installed as 265th Pope at Outdoor Mass
2005-04-25.abstract: Benedict XVI is installed as 265th pope of Roman Catholic Church in huge outdoor Mass in St Peter's Square; ceremonies described; photo (M)

2005-04-25.title: White House Letter: Tending spring flowers in a president's garden

2005-04-26.title: World Briefing | Asia: May Likely For South Asian Summit Meeting
2005-04-26.abstract: The annual meeting of prime ministers from South Asian countries is most likely to be rescheduled for late May in the Bangladeshi capital, Dhaka, the Press Trust of India reported. The previously scheduled meeting of the group, the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation, was canceled in February when India refused to attend, citing insecurity in Bangladesh and King Gyanendra's seizure of power in Nepal.   Somini Sengupta (NYT)

2005-04-26.title: German Foreign Minister Faces Hearing on Criminal Immigrants
2005-04-26.abstract: Foreign Min Joschka Fischer of Germany, speaking at unusual televised hearing, admits mistakes were made in granting visas to Germany to thousands of criminals and prostitutes from Ukraine from 2000 to 2002; but he dismisses opposition argument that security and well-being of Germany were significantly harmed by influx of immigrants, and says once problem was discovered in 2002, policy was immediately corrected; says affair has been exaggerated by opposition looking for political gain (M)

2005-04-26.title: Names of the Dead
2005-04-26.abstract: Defense Deptconfirms deaths of three service members in Iraq (S)ÿ

2005-04-26.title: At least 50 die in Japan derailment
Iraqis Extend Negotiations Over Makeup of New Government

2005-04-27.title: Briefly: EU Parliament to drop issue of Barroso trip
Supreme Court sides with gun owner on foreign arrest
Briefly: Philadelphia takes title of most depressed city
Italians Angry Over Inquiry on Iraq Death
2005-04-27.abstract: Tensions between the United States and Italy surged Tuesday, as Italian politicians and citizens reacted furiously to leaked reports in the Italian news media that a joint investigation into the shooting death of an Italian agent in Baghdad would absolve American soldiers of guilt in the incident.    The United States ambassador to Rome, Mel Sembler, met twice with Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi and his top aide at the government's headquarters to try to avert a crisis that could cost the United States one of its staunchest European allies in the Iraq conflict.

2005-04-28.title: Egyptian Reformers Take to Streets to Oppose a 5th Term for Mubarak
2005-04-28.abstract: Egypt's fledgling political reform movement organizes demonstrations in 14 major cities; huge numbers of riot poilcemen and scores of detentions ensure gatherings are either minuscule or do not materialize; in Cairo, first protesters to arrive are forced into taxis and drivers are ordered to drive away; about 200 protesters, hemmed in by police, chant antigovernment slogans; their primary demand is that Pres Hosni Mubarak step aside after 24 years in power; photo (M)

2005-04-28.title: Blair, in Reversal, Releases Iraq Paper in Bid to Defuse Election Issue
Entr'acte:In Britain's campaign, the unmentionable arts
Blair fears backers will be no-shows

2005-04-29.title: Zapatero offers Spain's yes on EU charter as example
Europa: German 'second front' against Joschka Fischer
World Briefing | Asia: India: U.S. Adds Insurgent Group To Terror List
2005-04-29.abstract: The State Department added to its list of foreign terrorist organizations an ethnic insurgency group based in northeastern Assam State. The rebel group, United Liberation Front of Assam, is considered the largest of several separatist organizations that operate in the northeast and accuse the Indian government of neglect. The United States and India have significantly expanded cooperation on counterterrorism efforts since Sept. 11, 2001. Insurgents fighting over Kashmir are already on the American terrorist list.   Somini Sengupta (NYT)

2005-04-29.title: Iraq's Assembly Accepts Cabinet Despite Tension
2005-04-29.abstract: Iraq's National Assembly votes overwhelmingly to approve Shiite-led cabinet, creating first fully and freely elected government in Iraqi history; divisions that delayed government's formation for three months after elections become apparent immediately; leader of Shiite political alliance that dominates assembly and cabinet delivers fiery speech hinting at purges to come in government's security forces; Sunni assembly member then accuses Shiites of dividing country; Sunni Arabs, who dominated Iraq's government under Saddam Hussein, largely boycotted Jan elections; now they say they are being 'marginalized' by 'government of winners'; cabinet remains incomplete; assembly's political factions have not yet agreed on candidates for five important ministries--defense, oil, electricity, industry and human rights; new government, when it assumes power early next week, will face extraordinary array of challenges, from guiding effort to write new constitution to rebuilding Iraq's shattered cities and fighting undiminished insurgency; photo; chart comparing positions held by minority groups and women in interim and transitional governments (M)

2005-04-29.title: Rebels Respond to New Iraqi Coalition With Wave of Attacks
2005-04-29.abstract: Ten car bombs across greater Baghdad killed at least 30 people, mostly members of the Iraqi forces, and wounded 99 others.

2005-04-30.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Yemen: 18 New Cases Of Polio Found
2005-04-30.abstract: Eighteen new cases of polio have been found in Yemen, and the country is ''now facing a major epidemic,'' Dr. David L. Heymann, the chief of polio eradication for the World Health Organization, said in Geneva. Four cases were identified in the Red Sea port of Al Hudaida on April 20, but the new ones have been found across the country, including in the capital, Sana. Yemen had been polio-free since 1996. Dr. Bruce Aylward, who coordinates the W.H.O. eradication campaign, said testing ''quite clearly'' showed that the strain had arrived recently and had come from Kano, in Nigeria, by way of Chad and Port Sudan, across the Red Sea. Yemen, worried about outbreaks in Sudan and Saudi Arabia, held a vaccination day for children this month, and more are planned. Health authorities are using a new monovalent oral vaccine that needs fewer doses to create immunity to type 1 polio, the strain circulating in Yemen.   Donald G. McNeil Jr. (NYT)

2005-04-30.title: Briefly: ETA suspects had list that might be of targets
Russia is an advocate, Putin tells Palestinians
Abduction, Often Violent, a Kyrgyz Wedding Rite
2005-04-30.abstract: When Ainur Tairova realized she was on her way to her wedding, she started choking the driver.    Her marriage was intended to be to a man she had met only the day before, and briefly at that. Several of his friends had duped her into getting into a car; they picked up the would-be groom and then headed for his home.

2005-04-30.title: Wave of Attacks in Iraq Kill 40 and Wound 100
2005-04-30.abstract: Insurgents determined to destabilize Iraq's new government executed a devastating series of coordinated attacks on Iraqi forces on Friday, detonating 12 car bombs across greater Baghdad and striking military targets throughout Iraq. At least 40 people were killed and more than 100 others wounded.    The attacks, a direct challenge to the new Shiite-dominated government that was formed Thursday, were aimed at Iraqi police officers and national guardsmen at their bases and traveling in convoys in northern and southern Baghdad and in Madaen, 15 miles southeast of the capital. At least 23 Iraqi policemen and troops were killed. Some reports put the total death toll at as many as 50 people.
